original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless CL gauge creation can drain/brick GaugeFactoryCL rewardToken balance via repeated createEternalFarming seeding
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() is externally callable without access control and unconditionally calls createEternalFarming() which approves and attempts to seed an AlgebraEternalFarming incentive with a fixed reward amount (1e10). If the factory is pre-funded with rewardToken (likely BLACK), an attacker can repeatedly create gauges to consume the factory’s rewardToken balance (or repeatedly trigger reverts once partially depleted), disrupting future legitimate CL gauge creation.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a practical permissionless entry point that can be abused on-chain without MEV. If the factory holds reward tokens to make CL gauge creation work, the seeding transfer becomes an attacker-controlled sink. Even if it reverts when insufficient, it can be used to permanently block CL gauge creation (DoS) if the protocol relies on keeping the factory funded and open.
deployment_assumptions: GaugeFactoryCL holds/receives rewardToken balances to seed farming incentives (or has some mechanism to fund createEternalFarming)
GaugeManager/GaugeFactoryCL are used in production with permissionless gauge creation enabled
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment script shown that restricts GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge; createEternalFarming seeds fixed reward=1e10 in code. Funding mechanism for factory is not shown.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createGauge() is external without access control; unconditionally calls createEternalFarming(); createEternalFarming uses fixed reward=1e10 and safeApprove to AlgebraEternalFarming

preconditions: Factory has a positive balance of _rewardToken (or approval-based pull is effective in AlgebraEternalFarming)
Attacker can supply pools that pass downstream creation checks (or creation does not validate pool authenticity)
AlgebraEternalFarmingCustom.createEternalFarming actually transfers/pulls the approved rewardToken from GaugeFactoryCL

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless external creation + fixed incentive seeding amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Repeated calls can consume factory token reserves or permanently revert gauge creation once reserves insufficient
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyAllowed/owner check on createGauge; no per-pool/per-deployer rate limit; no requirement that caller funds seeding

analysis_questions: Does AlgebraEternalFarmingCustom.createEternalFarming pull reward tokens from msg.sender (factory) using allowance?
Is GaugeFactoryCL expected to be pre-funded, and with which token(s)?
Are there any hidden constraints in GaugeManager._createGauge that prevent arbitrary pool addresses for CL gauges?
validation_requirements: Fork test: fund GaugeFactoryCL with rewardToken and repeatedly call createGauge to measure token depletion
Confirm call semantics of createEternalFarming in external Algebra contracts (transferFrom vs internal accounting)
Check if GaugeManager only ever calls factory for valid Algebra pools and whether attacker can bypass that via direct factory calls

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge(...) for many pools (or even invalid pools if not validated) until GaugeFactoryCL’s rewardToken balance is depleted, causing future gauge creations to fail or draining protocol-owned reward tokens into farming incentives controlled by the attacker’s chosen pool/deployer context.
prerequisites: Access to valid pool addresses satisfying create path
Factory holds rewardToken funds (or allowance/pull causes transfer)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of factory-held reward tokens and/or permanent disruption of CL gauge onboarding
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol operators and all users relying on new CL gauges and associated incentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin refilling factory funds or upgrading/reconfiguring factory access control

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for adversarial direct calls to GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() with factory-funded rewardToken

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Confirm per-call gas and feasibility of repeated calls within typical block limits
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Low if factory is already funded; otherwise attacker cost is gas only
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain observable via repeated GaugeCreated events / factory token balance drop

validation_result.mechanism: Missing access control on GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() + token drain via createEternalFarming seeding
validation_result.impact_summary: GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() is callable by anyone without access control. If the factory holds reward tokens, an attacker can repeatedly call it to drain those tokens. Even without tokens, the missing access control bypasses all validation in GaugeManager._createGauge.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() at GaugeFactoryCL.sol is an external function with no access control modifier. The intended call path is GaugeManager._createGauge() → GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge(), where GaugeManager performs critical validation (token whitelist, valid pair, duplicate gauge check, etc.). However, since GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() has no caller restriction, anyone can call it directly, bypassing all GaugeManager validation. Each call unconditionally invokes createEternalFarming() which approves 1e10 of the specified _rewardToken from the factory to the farming contract, and then calls createEternalFarming on the external contract (which presumably pulls those tokens). If the factory holds reward tokens, repeated calls drain them. The attacker also controls all parameters (including _rewardToken and farmingParam.algebraEternalFarming), which could allow draining arbitrary tokens held by the factory via a malicious algebraEternalFarming address. Other functions in the same contract (setDibs, setReferralFee, setInternalBribe) use onlyAllowed or EmergencyCouncil modifiers, making the absence of access control on createGauge clearly a defect rather than an intentional design choice.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at GaugeFactoryCL.sol lines 34-67 is correct. The createGauge function is external without any access control. The createEternalFarming internal function approves 1e10 of _rewardToken and calls the external AlgebraEternalFarming contract to create farming with that seed amount. The code evidence conclusively demonstrates the defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier (no onlyAllowed, no owner check, no msg.sender == gaugeManager check). Anyone can call this function directly, bypassing all validation in GaugeManager._createGauge and triggering token approvals/transfers from the factory.

Approves 1e10 of _rewardToken from the factory contract to _algebraEternalFarming, then calls createEternalFarming which presumably transfers those tokens. Since the caller controls all parameters (including _algebraEternalFarming address), a malicious caller could pass a contract they control to drain any tokens held by the factory.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() directly with attacker-controlled parameters
2. Function has no access control check - execution proceeds unconditionally
5. IAlgebraEternalFarmingCustom.createEternalFarming() is called, which pulls 1e10 tokens from factory via allowance (line 84)
6. Attacker repeats to drain factory balance, or factory reverts if insufficient balance (DoS)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists because GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() lacks any access control. Compare with other functions in the same contract: setDibs() and setReferralFee() use the 'onlyAllowed' modifier (requiring owner or GAUGE_ADMIN role), activateEmergencyMode() uses 'EmergencyCouncil', and setRegistry()/setAlgebraPoolApi() check 'owner() == msg.sender'. The createGauge() function, which is the most security-critical function that moves tokens, has none of these protections. The intended caller is GaugeManager (see GaugeManager.sol _createGauge function at approximately line 158: `_gauge = IGaugeFactoryCL(_gaugeFactory).createGauge(base, _ve, _pool, address(this), ...)`), which performs whitelist/pair validation before calling GaugeFactoryCL.

deployment_assumptions_checked: GaugeFactoryCL holds/receives rewardToken balances to seed farming incentives
GaugeManager/GaugeFactoryCL are used in production with permissionless gauge creation enabled
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts fund GaugeFactoryCL with reward tokens. The setReferralFeeInGaugeFactory.js script sets referral fee to 500, and setAlgebraPoolApiInGauge.js sets the API storage, but no script transfers reward tokens to the factory. However, the code at createEternalFarming clearly requires the factory to hold tokens (safeApprove + external call that would pull them), meaning either the factory must be funded post-deployment or CL gauge creation doesn't work at all.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid with two variants: (1) If factory is funded: attacker repeatedly calls createGauge with valid Algebra pool addresses (the pool needs to implement tickSpacing() and plugin()), draining 1e10 tokens per call. (2) If factory is NOT funded: the safeApprove may succeed (approving 0 or reverting depending on implementation) but the createEternalFarming call on the external contract will revert when trying to pull tokens, making all CL gauge creation permanently DoS'd. (3) More critically: since the attacker controls farmingParam.algebraEternalFarming, they can pass a malicious contract that calls transferFrom on the factory for the approved amount, stealing tokens even if the real AlgebraEternalFarming would reject the call. The missing access control is the root cause in all variants.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The missing access control on GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() is a clear code defect. Impact: (1) Token drain if factory is funded, (2) DoS of CL gauge creation if factory isn't funded, (3) Bypasses all GaugeManager validation (whitelist, pair validity, duplicate gauge checks), (4) Attacker controls all parameters including the farming contract address, enabling arbitrary token approval theft. Even without profit extraction, the code is defective because it lacks an obviously-intended access control check.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() to restrict callers to GaugeManager only. For example: add a state variable `address public gaugeManager` set during initialization, and add `require(msg.sender == gaugeManager)` to createGauge().
Alternatively, use the existing onlyAllowed modifier pattern to restrict to owner or GAUGE_ADMIN role.
Consider having the caller (GaugeManager) transfer the seed tokens to the factory before calling createGauge, rather than requiring the factory to be pre-funded.
Add a check that _algebraEternalFarming matches the expected contract address to prevent arbitrary approval targets.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GaugeFactoryCL and initialize. 2. Fund it with reward tokens (e.g., 1e15). 3. Deploy a mock AlgebraPool implementing tickSpacing() and plugin(). 4. Call createGauge directly on GaugeFactoryCL from an arbitrary EOA, passing the mock pool and real/mock algebraEternalFarming. 5. Observe that 1e10 tokens are approved and transferred. 6. Repeat to measure depletion. 7. Alternatively, pass a malicious _algebraEternalFarming that calls transferFrom on the factory to steal the approved tokens.
uncertainties: Whether IAlgebraEternalFarmingCustom.createEternalFarming actually pulls tokens via transferFrom (source not in scope), though the pattern of approve-then-call strongly implies it does
Whether the factory is actually funded in production (deployment scripts are silent on this)
Whether the external AlgebraEternalFarming contract has its own validation that might reject calls from unexpected callers or with invalid pool addresses
additional_testing_needed: Test direct call to GaugeFactoryCL.createGauge() from non-GaugeManager address to confirm no revert
Test with funded factory to confirm token depletion per call
Test with malicious _algebraEternalFarming to confirm arbitrary token drain via approval
Test DoS scenario: fund factory with < 1e10 tokens and confirm CL gauge creation reverts
